title: Abuse: Aiming for Biblical Balance (Part 1)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 02-18-24 - Abuse_ Aiming for Biblical Balance - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Three Foundational Agreements
subpoints: Marriage is worth protecting — what God has joined together let no man separate (Matt. 19:6)
Some believers are called to stay in difficult marriages — 1 Cor. 7; 1 Peter 3:1
Victims of oppression must be protected — some marriages are so oppressive that safety requires separation
The church has historically failed to adequately protect victims and hold perpetrators accountable
Going beyond Scripture in either direction hurts both the abused and the abuser

outline.title: Overstatement 1: 'Always Believe the Victim'
subpoints: Valid concern: claims of abuse have been dismissed because the perpetrator seemed respectable, or because others didn't want to get involved
Danger: automatically treating the accused as guilty violates the biblical standard — 'by two or three witnesses shall a matter be established' (Deut. 19:15)
False accusations do occur and can destroy a person's reputation and freedom — Joseph and Potiphar's wife (Gen. 39)
Better approach: take all claims seriously, ensure safety, report to civil authorities, but do not treat the accused as guilty without evidence

outline.title: Overstatement 2: 'If You Feel Abused, You Were Abused'
subpoints: Valid concern: abusers often manipulate victims into feeling guilty for what happened; victims may be reluctant to come forward
Danger: makes abuse entirely subjective; a person who is very self-centered will interpret everything as abuse
The Bible teaches that it is possible to wrongly interpret the words, actions, and motives of others — Eli and Hannah (1 Sam. 1)
Better approach: help the person objectively evaluate what happened, consider pattern and cumulative effect, distinguish one-time incidents from ongoing patterns

outline.title: Overstatement 3: 'Victims Bear No Fault at Any Point'
subpoints: Valid concern: abusers often blame the victim ('she provoked me'); victims suffer false guilt thinking they deserved it
The abuser is 100% solely guilty for the abuse — there is no valid excuse for physical or sexual abuse (Romans 12:18-21)
Danger: denies the doctrine of total depravity; victims may also have sins (bitterness, desire for revenge, patterns of behavior) that need pastoral care
Better approach: affirm the abuser's complete guilt; at an appropriate time help the victim grow in grace, forgiveness, and dealing with their own sinful responses

outline.title: Overstatement 4: 'If You Feel Mistreated or Controlled, Leave'
subpoints: Valid concern: many women stay in genuinely dangerous situations when they have every right to seek safety
Danger: fails to distinguish between degrees of sinful mistreatment — a self-centered or mean husband is not the same as a violent one
The popular label 'narcissist' is being used as a get-out-of-jail-free card for divorce without biblical warrant

Better approach: affirm the right to safety for the genuinely abused; exercise wisdom in distinguishing degrees of sin; a man who refuses to repent may go through church discipline which provides time and space to reveal or change his heart

summary: The first lesson in a multi-part Sunday school series on abuse. Pastor Tito Lyro opens by establishing three foundational agreements all Bible-believing Christians should share: marriage is worth protecting, some believers are called to stay in difficult marriages, and victims of oppression must be protected. He then examines four of eight common 'overstatements' in responding to abuse — positions that are well-intentioned but go beyond what Scripture teaches: always believe the victim, if you feel abused you were abused, victims bear no fault at any point, and if you feel controlled leave the relationship. For each, he identifies the valid concern behind it, the danger of the overstatement, and a more biblically balanced approach.
transcript:
## Introduction: A Biblical Balance on Abuse

We just finished a series on parenting, and we recently had two messages from 1 Peter 3:1-7 on the roles of husband and wife — emphasizing the wife's submission and the husband's authority. Because marriage and parenting are two contexts in which abuse is more prone to happen, your elders thought it would be a good idea to take a few Sunday school periods to talk about abuse.

Our goal is to aim for a biblical balance on this subject. Jim Newheiser, a professor at RTS Charlotte, wrote a little book called *The Abuse Pendulum*, describing how the church has swung from 'nothing is abuse' to the other extreme. Scripture tells us that being blown about by every wind of doctrine is a sign of immaturity. We want to ask: what do the Scriptures teach about abuse, and how can we reach a biblical balance?

We opened by reading Psalm 82, which speaks of God standing in judgment over those who fail to defend the poor and needy and deliver them from the wicked — a fitting frame for the subject.

## Three Foundational Agreements

Before looking at the overstatements, let me identify three things I believe all Bible-believing Christians should be able to agree on.

**1. Marriage is worth protecting.** Our Lord Jesus said, 'What God has joined together let no man separate.' Every marriage that ends, ends in affront to God's design. That is not to say that no marriage should ever end — but that every ending is a tragedy.

**2. Some believers are called to stay in difficult marriages.** Peter tells wives to win an unbelieving husband without words; Paul in 1 Corinthians 7 says the believing spouse is to remain unless the unbelieving spouse leaves. Some difficult marriages are worth persevering in.

**3. Victims of oppression must be protected.** Some marriages are so oppressive that those being injured have the freedom to remove themselves for safety. The sixth commandment — 'You shall not murder' — includes the preservation of life. Safety is a legitimate concern.

A fourth observation: the church has historically failed, at times, to adequately protect victims and hold perpetrators accountable. That is true. And at the same time, some segments of the church have overcorrected to the point of going beyond what Scripture teaches — and when we go beyond Scripture in either direction, we hurt both the abused and the abuser.

## Eight Overstatements in Responding to Abuse

We will examine eight statements that sound compassionate but are, on closer examination, overstatements. For each, I want to identify: (1) the valid concern behind it, (2) the danger of the overstatement, and (3) a better, more biblically balanced approach.

### Overstatement 1: Always Believe the Victim

This is the standard approach in abuse response today.

**Valid concern:** This statement arises out of sympathy for many victims whose claims have been dismissed because people could not imagine that the respected perpetrator had done such a thing. Or, frankly, because others did not want the messiness that follows when an allegation is taken seriously.

**Danger:** This statement automatically treats the accused as guilty without investigation. The Scriptures set a high standard of proof before treating someone as guilty: *One witness shall not rise against a man concerning any iniquity or any sin. By the mouth of two or three witnesses the matter shall be established* (Deut. 19:15). Witnesses can include more than testimony — medical evidence, photographs, a documented pattern of behavior — but the principle remains. Innocent people have been permanently damaged by false claims. Joseph and Potiphar's wife is the biblical example: a false accusation led to imprisonment.

**Better approach:** Take all claims of abuse seriously. Do not dismiss them. Offer compassionate care and ensure the safety of the threatened party. Report to the civil authorities, who have tools to investigate that we do not. But do not treat the accused as guilty without proof.

### Overstatement 2: If You Feel Abused, You Were Abused

**Valid concern:** Abusers are often masters of manipulation. They can make the abused person feel guilty for what happened. This statement is trying to say: even if the abuser does not acknowledge his behavior as abusive, there may be real truth in the allegation.

**Danger:** This makes abuse entirely subjective — it is not what happened but how you felt about it that matters. In a culture already prone to a victim mentality, where a raised eyebrow has been filed as an abuse claim on a college campus, this is dangerous. The Bible teaches that it is possible to wrongly interpret the words, actions, and motives of others. Eli wrongly accused Hannah of being drunk when she was praying (1 Sam. 1:12-14). Love 'hopes all things,' which means it seeks to assume the best of others, not to interpret everything as abusive.

**Better approach:** Help the person who feels abused to objectively evaluate what happened. Consider the ongoing pattern and cumulative effect, as well as the specific incident. A one-time explosion with nothing before or after is different from an established pattern. Proper evaluation keeps us from wrongly escalating consequences for one incident while also not dismissing a genuine pattern.

### Overstatement 3: Victims of Abuse Bear No Fault at Any Point

**Valid concern:** Many abusers blame their victims — 'she provoked me' or 'she failed to be a good enough wife.' Many victims suffer from false guilt, thinking the abuse was their fault. This statement is trying to say: no sin of the victim justifies the abuse.

**The essential truth:** The abuser is 100% solely guilty for the abuse itself. There is no valid excuse for physical or sexual abuse. Even if an abuser believes he was provoked, he is never authorized by God to take physical or verbal vengeance (Romans 12:18-21). A murdered person does not sin by being murdered; a genuinely abused person does not sin by being abused.

**Danger:** This overstatement denies total depravity — the victim is also a sinner. Not guilty of the abuse, but there may be other things in the victim's life that need pastoral care: bitterness, desire for revenge, patterns of behavior that need to be addressed in her walk with the Lord. These are not addressed as justification for the abuse but as part of helping her grow.

**Better approach:** Firmly affirm that the abuse is never justified. At an appropriate time — not the first thing — help the victim examine herself in light of Scripture, dealing with bitterness, forgiveness, and growth. The goal is her flourishing in Christ, not to excuse her abuser.

### Overstatement 4: If You Feel Mistreated or Controlled, Leave the Relationship

**Valid concern:** Many women remain in genuinely dangerous and abusive situations when they have every right to seek safety. The threshold for when to seek safety will be different for each person, and we must not set an artificially high bar.

**Danger:** This fails to distinguish between degrees of sinful mistreatment. A mean or self-centered husband is not the same as a violent one. The popular label 'narcissist' — meaning selfish, self-centered — is being applied so broadly that it has become a de facto permission slip for divorce without biblical warrant.

**Better approach:** Affirm the right of the genuinely abused to seek safety. Exercise wisdom in distinguishing degrees of sin in a relationship. A man who refuses to repent of controlling and angry behavior may go through a process of church discipline, which gives time for his heart to reveal itself or for the Lord to change him. We preserve marriages at all biblical costs, not at all costs — but we preserve them.
